headline: PETRONAS Upstream Capex Cuts 22% Amid Energy Transition Pressure
context: Petronas reduced upstream capital expenditure by 22% to RM28 billion in 2025, prioritising energy transition investments over new oil and gas exploration, raising concerns about long-term supply security and employment in petroleum-dependent states.

cards.big: Petronas cuts RM8 billion from upstream oil spending — energy transition is real but 22% of national revenue still comes from petroleum
cards.sub: Reducing exploration now means production declines accelerate from 2028 — the transition gap could hit before alternatives are ready

cards.big: Malaysia's proven oil reserves at 3.6 billion barrels support only 14 years of production at current rates
cards.sub: Without new exploration, production falls 8% annually from depletion — RM12 billion revenue loss by 2030 if no new fields found

cards.big: 48,000 workers in upstream petroleum face gradual job reduction — retraining programmes cover only 12% of affected workforce
cards.sub: Terengganu and Sabah communities built around petroleum supply bases have no alternative economic anchor

cards.big: Petronas allocated RM12 billion to renewables and hydrogen — but these investments won't generate revenue comparable to oil until 2035
cards.sub: 10-year revenue gap between petroleum decline and renewable maturity creates fiscal planning nightmare

cards.big: The energy transition is necessary — but cutting oil investment before renewables can pay the bills risks a decade of fiscal crisis
Should Petronas maintain upstream spending at 2023 levels until renewable energy revenue reaches 15% of petroleum income?
